title: Computer vision and perception

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Computer vision and perception; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Perception and application areas: language, vision, robotics, and integrated systems; atomic topic: Computer vision and perception

canonicalExplanation.summary: Computer vision maps sensed pixel arrays through representations to tasks such as classification, detection, or segmentation; labels and evaluation are conditional on capture conditions and decision policy.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A defect detector is tested by camera, lighting, angle, product type, and defect size, with missed-critical-defect rate prioritized over headline accuracy. Your goal is to design an evaluation plan that includes lighting, viewpoint and subgroup conditions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Neural networks and learned representations; Knowledge representation and uncertainty; Convolution, sequence and attention roles; Sampling, labels and data quality. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Computer vision maps sensed pixel arrays through representations to tasks such as classification, detection, or segmentation; labels and evaluation are conditional on capture conditions and decision policy. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Pixels omit physical context, lighting/viewpoint/sensor changes shift data, labels can be subjective, aggregate accuracy hides slices, adversarial or accidental artifacts can dominate, and perception is not causal understanding. The learner must reject this overclaim: High test accuracy proves a vision model recognizes the intended object features and will remain reliable under ordinary camera changes.

includes: Relate pixels, features, detection, classification and segmentation to sensing conditions and label policy.
Design an evaluation plan that includes lighting, viewpoint and subgroup conditions.
excludes: Pixels omit physical context, lighting/viewpoint/sensor changes shift data, labels can be subjective, aggregate accuracy hides slices, adversarial or accidental artifacts can dominate, and perception is not causal understanding.

outcomes: Design an evaluation plan that includes lighting, viewpoint and subgroup conditions.

examples.title: Computer vision and perception: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A defect detector is tested by camera, lighting, angle, product type, and defect size, with missed-critical-defect rate prioritized over headline accuracy.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A defect detector is tested by camera, lighting, angle, product type, and defect size, with missed-critical-defect rate prioritized over headline accuracy.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Evaluate sensing conditions, shortcuts, subgroup and rare-case slices, calibration, and operational failure costs; pixel prediction does not prove intended perception.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Evaluate sensing conditions, shortcuts, subgroup and rare-case slices, calibration, and operational failure costs; pixel prediction does not prove intended perception.

examples.title: Computer vision and perception: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A model keys on a background marker correlated with class in training, so occlusion and changed-background tests expose shortcut learning.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A model keys on a background marker correlated with class in training, so occlusion and changed-background tests expose shortcut learning.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Pixels omit physical context, lighting/viewpoint/sensor changes shift data, labels can be subjective, aggregate accuracy hides slices, adversarial or accidental artifacts can dominate, and perception is not causal understanding.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: High test accuracy proves a vision model recognizes the intended object features and will remain reliable under ordinary camera changes.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design an evaluation plan that includes lighting, viewpoint and subgroup conditions.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: High test accuracy proves a vision model recognizes the intended object features and will remain reliable under ordinary camera changes.

limitations: more modalities or fluent outputs do not establish understanding, truth, physical safety, or universal coverage
